judgement_by: Hon. Dr. D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
keywords: The 5th Respondent Trade Union claiming to command 40% of the Petitioner’s employees as its members; therefore, claims to have a right to enter into a collective agreement with the Petitioner; the Petitioner disputes this fact. On a dispute arising on that, the 5th Respondent made a complaint to the 1st Respondent, Petitioner raised an objection on the maintainability of the complaint. In the meantime, Petitioner made a complaint to the 1st Respondent that the 5th Respondent creates adverse atmosphere that disturbs the operations and functions of the Petitioner factory. However, without taking a decision on both said complaints, on a purported complaint made by the 5th Respondent subsequently, the 2nd Respondent took a decision to go for a poll which is challenged in this Application on the basis that the Petitioner entertained legitimate expectation. Held – writs issued.
legistation: The Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka

keywords: The Petitioner seeks to obtain a Writ of Mandamus against the 1st Respondent for not taking steps to issue the FL-04 liquor license that the Petitioner is entitled to. The 1st Petitioner, who had the liquor license and ran the business from 1971 to 1995, was later denied the issuance of an application form to renew his license and thereby, his license lapsed. However, the Petitioners made another application in 2023 to obtain the FL-04 license, which was accepted by the 1st Respondent, but sought the permission of the 3rd Respondent to proceed with the issuance of the same. Held – Writ issued.

keywords: The Petitioner had omitted to mention goods imported in relation to one commercial invoice in the Customs Declaration; therefore, Customs registered a case against the Petitioner and held an inquiry. At the inquiry, it was relieved that there was no intention on the part of the Petitioner to defraud the Customs; since there was a failure on the part of the Petitioner, goods were forfeited in terms of Section 47 of the Customs Ordinance. The Petitioner challenged the same. Held – Customs should not have acted under Section 47; instead, should have acted under Section 18(2); writs issued.

keywords: The Petitioner claims to be in possession of the parcel of land in suit which is part of a larger land released to the Sri Lanka Sugar Corporation under the State Industrial Corporations Act, No. 49 of 1957, by the State. The 2nd Respondent is alleged to have carved out a portion of the Petitioner’s land and forcibly occupy the same; the 1st Respondent is alleged to have issued a permit in respect of the same. Petitioner seeks to quash the issuance of such permit. Held – since the Petitioner cannot possess any land which had been released to the Sugar Corporation for the sole purpose of sugar cane cultivation as per the Supreme Court judgement, and the Petitioner’s Application is speculative, no writ lies.
legistation: The Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka
